sourceDocuments.title: Best practice management guidelines for fibrous dysplasia/McCune-Albright syndrome: a consensus statement from the FD/MAS international consortium.
sourceDocuments.authors: Javaid MK, Boyce A, Appelman-Dijkstra N, Ong J, Defabianis P, Offiah A, Arundel P, Shaw N
sourceDocuments.scope: International best-practice statements for definition, diagnosis, staging, treatment, monitoring, and multidisciplinary care of FD/MAS.

sourceDocuments.title: Fibrous dysplasia in children and its management.
sourceDocuments.authors: Gun ZH, Arif A, Boyce AM
sourceDocuments.scope: Current review of diagnosis and management challenges in children, including growth-related progression, surgery, physiatric care, endocrinopathies, bisphosphonates, and denosumab.
sourceDocuments.updatesNote: Updates pediatric management since the 2019 consensus by emphasizing that bisphosphonates may help pain but do not alter lesion progression and by highlighting emerging denosumab evidence needing further study.

sourceDocuments.title: Craniofacial Fibrous Dysplasia: Clinical and Therapeutic Implications.
sourceDocuments.authors: Szymczuk V, Taylor J, Boyce AM
sourceDocuments.scope: Focused current review of diagnosis, complications, and clinical management for craniofacial FD/MAS.

sourceDocuments.title: Clinical guidelines for the management of craniofacial fibrous dysplasia.
sourceDocuments.authors: Lee JS, FitzGibbon EJ, Chen YR, Kim HJ, Lustig LR, Akintoye SO, Collins MT, Kaban LB
sourceDocuments.scope: Clinically focused craniofacial FD guideline covering natural progression, diagnostic evaluation, multidisciplinary management, and research needs.

sourceDocuments.scope: Authoritative review of FD/MAS pathogenesis, age-related clinical features, evaluation, management, and unmet needs.

sourceDocuments.title: Fibrous Dysplasia / McCune-Albright Syndrome

synthesis.title: Fibrous Dysplasia — synthesis of the guidelines
synthesis.basedOn: Combined by AI from 7 source documents on the shelf.
synthesis.synthDisclaimer: This summary was prepared by AI from the source documents on the shelf — it is not an official guideline and may contain inaccuracies. Every claim links to the document it came from; read straight from the source if you prefer.

whatToDoNow.body: A DNA test for the GNAS mutation is decisive — histopathology alone is not enough.
CT or MRI shows how far the lesion reaches. A bone scan is done once at diagnosis, not on a cycle.

whatToDoNow.lead: Reach a specialist who knows this disease.
whatToDoNow.body: Not every surgeon or endocrinologist has seen it — check the doctor list on the disease page.
Reviews every 6–12 months; treatment is driven by pain and progression, not by the diagnosis alone.

redFlags.title: When to seek a second opinion
items: A recommendation to excise a child's fibrous dysplasia without a compelling reason (vision loss, severe deformity).
A histopathological diagnosis made without a confirmatory DNA test.
No consultation with an international consortium (FD/MAS Alliance / Leiden).

sections.title: 1. Diagnosis
sections.intro: Diagnosis of fibrous dysplasia/McCune-Albright syndrome is established by integrating clinical pattern, characteristic imaging, and selective histopathologic or molecular confirmation.

paragraphs.text: FD/MAS should be considered a mosaic GNAS-related disorder with a wide clinical spectrum, ranging from isolated skeletal disease to involvement of multiple bones with skin and/or endocrine features. At diagnosis, the practical aim is to define whether disease is monostotic or polyostotic and whether extraskeletal manifestations of McCune-Albright syndrome are present, because variable presentation across specialties can lead to misdiagnosis and unnecessary variation in investigations.

Imaging has a central role in positive diagnosis, differential diagnosis, prognosis, and follow-up of FD/MAS. A standardized radiologic approach is recommended, with radiologists actively contributing to recognition of typical bone lesions and communication with the wider clinical team; this helps avoid inconsistent work-up in a rare condition with multiple possible presentations.

source.loc: Imaging recommendations: diagnosis and differential diagnosis

paragraphs.text: For suspected craniofacial FD, diagnosis should account for the distinctive functional and anatomic complexity of the craniofacial skeleton. Current craniofacial review guidance emphasizes assessing potential complications and clinical implications alongside diagnosis, rather than treating craniofacial lesions as isolated radiographic findings.

update.note: Provides a current review of diagnosis, complications, and management implications for craniofacial FD/MAS, updating earlier craniofacial guideline framing.

paragraphs.text: In children, diagnosis should be interpreted in the context of skeletal growth and age-related lesion progression: fibro-osseous lesions expand during childhood and generally reach final disease burden in early adulthood. This pediatric context is important at diagnosis so that lesion activity and associated hyperfunctioning endocrinopathies are assessed without assuming adult disease behavior.

update.note: Adds child-specific diagnostic context related to growth, age-related lesion progression, and pediatric endocrine assessment.

paragraphs.text: Histopathology or genetic confirmation may support the diagnosis when clinical and imaging findings are uncertain, but diagnosis is generally framed around the mosaic nature of disease and the distribution of affected tissues. Because phenotype is determined by the location and extent of affected tissues, evaluation at diagnosis should focus on defining clinically relevant skeletal and extraskeletal involvement rather than pursuing unnecessary testing when the presentation is characteristic.

sections.title: 2. Histopathology and genetics
sections.intro: Histopathology and genetics can support the diagnosis of fibrous dysplasia/McCune-Albright syndrome, but both have important limits in routine patient care.

paragraphs.text: FD/MAS is defined biologically as a disorder caused by somatic gain-of-function mutations of the GNAS gene, producing overactivity in affected tissues and a broad phenotype that varies by severity and age of onset. Because the disease is mosaic, clinical expression depends on which tissues are involved; genetic confirmation may therefore support the diagnosis but should be interpreted in the context of the patient’s clinical and radiologic findings.

Histopathology explains the lesion process: activating post-zygotic GNAS mutations inhibit normal differentiation and proliferation of bone-forming stromal cells, leading to replacement of normal bone and marrow by fibrous tissue and woven bone. Tissue examination may be useful when diagnosis is uncertain or when alternative pathology must be excluded, but histology is one component of a multidisciplinary diagnostic evaluation rather than a stand-alone pathway for every patient.

A more recent pathophysiologic review frames FD/MAS as a rare mosaic disease of Gαs activation, in which inappropriate cyclic AMP signaling in bone impairs skeletal stem-cell differentiation and produces discrete skeletal lesions prone to fracture, deformity, and pain. This mosaic biology helps explain why genetic testing can be limited by tissue sampling: a negative result from an unaffected or low-burden sample does not necessarily exclude FD/MAS when the clinical picture is otherwise typical.

update.note: Broadens the post-zygotic GNAS framework with a mosaic Gαs/cAMP pathophysiology model; complements rather than supersedes the craniofacial consensus.

paragraphs.text: In children, FD is described as a mosaic disorder arising from somatic Gαs variants, with impaired osteogenic cell differentiation; fibro-osseous lesions expand during childhood and typically reach final disease burden in early adulthood. This age-related evolution means that histopathologic or genetic information should be integrated with growth-related clinical and imaging changes, rather than used in isolation to predict future burden or guide all management decisions.

update.note: Adds child-specific emphasis on somatic Gαs variants, skeletal growth, and age-related lesion progression.

sections.title: 3. Therapy
sections.intro: Treatment for fibrous dysplasia/McCune-Albright syndrome is individualized by disease site, symptoms, age, and associated endocrinopathies, and should be coordinated by clinicians experienced in FD/MAS.

paragraphs.text: Therapy is indicated when FD/MAS causes pain, fracture risk, deformity, functional impairment, craniofacial complications, or clinically important extraskeletal disease. Care should be organized through a multidisciplinary team, with escalation to specialist FD/MAS services for complex, progressive, craniofacial, endocrine, or surgically challenging disease. Management aims to reduce symptoms and complications, support function, and monitor for change rather than to eradicate lesions.

In children, treatment decisions should account for skeletal growth and the tendency for fibro-osseous lesions to expand during childhood and reach final disease burden in early adulthood. The main treatment pillars are surgical correction of skeletal deformities when needed, physiatric and rehabilitation care to preserve function, and specialist medical management of associated hyperfunctioning endocrinopathies. Children with progressive deformity, fractures, pain, or endocrine features should be managed by pediatric teams with relevant orthopedic, endocrine, rehabilitation, and craniofacial expertise.

update.note: Adds child-specific emphasis on growth-related progression, physiatric care, and pediatric multidisciplinary decision-making.

paragraphs.text: Medical therapy for skeletal disease should be led by a specialist and not initiated from a web summary. Bisphosphonates may be considered for FD-related bone pain, but current pediatric review evidence states that they do not alter lesion quality or progression. Denosumab is an emerging option that may improve lesion activity and mineralization, but further studies are needed before its routine role—particularly in children—can be defined; use therefore requires expert supervision and careful risk–benefit discussion.

update.note: Clarifies that bisphosphonates are for pain rather than lesion modification, and frames denosumab as investigational/emerging rather than routine therapy.

paragraphs.text: Craniofacial FD requires site-specific management because of the functional and anatomical complexity of the skull and face. Observation may be appropriate for stable disease, while escalation to craniofacial, otolaryngology, ophthalmology, neurosurgical, dental, or maxillofacial specialists is warranted for symptoms, cosmetic or structural progression, sensory compromise, or other complications. Treatment planning should balance functional protection, deformity management, and the natural history of craniofacial lesions.

Imaging supports therapy by defining disease extent, prognosis, complications, and follow-up needs, and by improving communication between radiologists and treating specialists. Imaging-guided reassessment is particularly important when symptoms change, complications are suspected, surgery is being considered, or ongoing surveillance is needed for potentially disabling disease.

sections.title: 4. Indications for surgery
sections.intro: Surgery in fibrous dysplasia/McCune-Albright syndrome is reserved for defined clinical problems rather than for radiographic disease alone.

paragraphs.text: The base consensus frames management as individualized and multidisciplinary because FD/MAS has a wide clinical spectrum and limited high-strength evidence. Within this approach, surgery is considered when lesions produce clinically important consequences such as pain not otherwise controlled, fracture or impending structural compromise, progressive deformity, or functional impairment; the aim is to address the patient’s symptoms, stability, function, and safety rather than to remove all affected bone.

Surgery is not indicated solely because FD is visible on imaging or because a lesion is present. The consensus emphasizes diagnosis, staging, treatment, monitoring, and multidisciplinary care to avoid inappropriate variability in investigations and treatments; stable or asymptomatic disease is generally managed with observation, surveillance, pain management, rehabilitation, and treatment of associated endocrine drivers rather than prophylactic operations without a compelling clinical indication.

In children, the threshold for surgery is shaped by skeletal growth and age-related lesion progression. Current pediatric guidance describes the mainstay of treatment as surgical correction of skeletal deformities, physiatric care, and medical management of associated hyperfunctioning endocrinopathies; therefore surgery is most compelling when deformity is clinically significant or progressive, threatens ambulation or function, or is associated with fracture risk, while medical and rehabilitative care remain central and bisphosphonates for pain do not alter lesion progression.

For craniofacial FD, indications for surgery are driven by functional and anatomical consequences in the face and skull. Because the craniofacial skeleton has unique functional complexity, operative intervention is considered when disease causes or threatens clinically important complications such as sensory, orbital, airway, dental/occlusal, or other functional compromise, or when deformity produces substantial clinical burden; otherwise management relies on careful diagnosis, monitoring, and multidisciplinary review.

The craniofacial guideline highlights that FD is non-malignant, variable in behavior, and difficult to manage because progression differs between patients. Decisions about surgery should therefore be made in a multidisciplinary setting after diagnostic evaluation and assessment of natural progression, with the rationale clearly tied to symptoms, functional risk, deformity, or complications rather than to the diagnosis alone.

sections.title: 5. Monitoring and follow-up
sections.intro: Follow-up in fibrous dysplasia/McCune-Albright syndrome should be individualized to disease extent, age, symptoms, and involved sites, with attention to skeletal and extraskeletal complications.

paragraphs.text: The international consensus guideline frames monitoring as part of ongoing multidisciplinary care, because FD/MAS varies widely in severity, age of onset, and the organs involved. Patient follow-up should therefore track symptoms, functional impact, skeletal complications, endocrine features when present, and changes that might alter treatment or referral needs.

Imaging is used not only for diagnosis but also for prognosis and follow-up. The imaging schedule should be standardized enough to allow comparison over time, but tailored to clinical presentation, lesion location, and the need to evaluate pain, deformity, fracture risk, or other complications.

Children require closer longitudinal surveillance because FD lesions expand during childhood and disease burden generally becomes established by early adulthood. Follow-up should monitor growth-related progression, skeletal deformity, pain, function, fractures, and associated hyperfunctioning endocrinopathies, with review frequency guided by age, skeletal growth, symptoms, and lesion activity.

update.note: Adds emphasis on childhood growth-related progression and pediatric management challenges.

paragraphs.text: For craniofacial FD, follow-up should reflect the variable natural history and the functional complexity of the skull and face. Surveillance should track clinical progression and complications relevant to craniofacial involvement, and should be coordinated through multidisciplinary care when symptoms, anatomy, or function require specialist input.

Ongoing assessment should recognize the age-related clinical and radiographic changes of FD/MAS and the broad clinical spectrum caused by mosaic tissue involvement. Follow-up is therefore not a single fixed schedule for all patients; it should be adjusted over time according to clinical course, skeletal lesion behavior, pain, deformity, fracture history, and extraskeletal manifestations.

suggestions.title: Specify denosumab pain-efficacy and stopping-risk counseling
suggestions.summary: When denosumab is considered for FD/MAS, discuss it as a non-routine specialist option with emerging evidence for clinically meaningful pain improvement, but require explicit counseling and planning for adverse events and discontinuation.
suggestions.rationale: Current guidance only states that denosumab is an emerging option that may improve lesion activity and mineralization and needs expert supervision. New systematic review data specifically quantify pain outcomes in FD/MAS: among 86 treated patients, mean pain score fell by 3.51/10, 81.8% were pain responders, and 51.7% reduced or stopped analgesics. The same review and broader rare-bone-disease evidence also identify clinically important treatment and post-treatment risks, especially hypocalcemia, hypophosphatemia, and rebound hypercalcemia after discontinuation, so the update should add explicit pain-efficacy counseling and a discontinuation safety plan rather than implying routine use.
suggestions.parentText: Newer 2025 reviews suggest the bone-strengthening drug denosumab can meaningfully reduce FD pain for many people, but it is a specialist-only option that needs careful counseling and a clear plan for stopping it safely — worth asking your specialist about.

suggestions.title: Add denosumab-specific biochemical follow-up
suggestions.summary: For patients receiving or stopping denosumab, follow-up should include planned calcium and phosphate monitoring during treatment and after discontinuation, with specialist oversight for rebound hypercalcemia risk.
suggestions.rationale: The existing monitoring section individualizes surveillance by disease extent, age, symptoms, imaging, and endocrine features, but it does not address treatment-specific biochemical monitoring. Recent denosumab reviews report on-treatment hypocalcemia and hypophosphatemia and post-discontinuation rebound hypercalcemia, including severe cases in FD/MAS, making this a new monitoring requirement for the subgroup exposed to denosumab.
suggestions.parentText: If denosumab is used, recent evidence says blood calcium and phosphate should be checked on a planned schedule during treatment and especially after stopping, because levels can rebound — a question to raise with the treating specialist.

suggestions.title: Record objective response measures when using denosumab
suggestions.summary: If denosumab is used, document baseline and follow-up pain scores, analgesic use, bone turnover markers, and imaging-based lesion activity where available to support individualized continuation or discontinuation decisions.
suggestions.rationale: Current therapy guidance says imaging supports reassessment and that denosumab is emerging, but it does not specify outcome measures for denosumab-treated patients. Recent systematic reviews report changes in pain scores, analgesic requirements, circulating bone markers, and Na[18F]F PET-CT lesion activity, indicating that structured objective response tracking is useful when this non-routine therapy is attempted.
suggestions.parentText: When denosumab is tried, it helps to track concrete measures over time — pain scores, painkiller use, bone blood markers, and scans — so the specialist can judge whether to continue or stop. Worth asking how response will be measured.

flagNotes.text: Add that a negative blood GNAS does NOT rule out FD — a first-contact reader could misread it.
The denosumab summary is faithful to Gun 2024, but state more clearly that a specialist leads it — not the family doctor.

synthesis.title: McCune-Albright Syndrome — synthesis of the guidelines
synthesis.basedOn: Combined from the FD/MAS consensus (MAS section) and the GeneReviews reference chapter.
synthesis.synthDisclaimer: This summary was prepared by AI from the source documents — it is not an official guideline and may contain inaccuracies. Show it to your doctor.

sections.title: 1. Diagnosis and screening
sections.intro: The classic triad: FD + café-au-lait macules + endocrinopathy. Two of the three are sufficient.

paragraphs.text: A diagnosis of MAS requires: (a) documented FD (CT + GNAS), (b) café-au-lait macules of the “coast of Maine” type with a border running along the body midline, (c) one of the endocrinopathies: GnRH-independent precocious puberty, hyperthyroidism, GH excess, hypercortisolism, or FGF23-mediated hypophosphatemia.

suggestions.title: Shorten the Tanner-staging interval in MAS girls aged 2–7
suggestions.summary: Shorten Tanner staging from 12 to 6 months in girls aged 2–7 — the peak-risk window for GnRH-independent precocious puberty.
suggestions.rationale: A screening-interval change with a monitoring downside (not therapy); stays clinician-only until it gathers more signal.

lines.tx: Annual Tanner staging in MAS girls aged 2–8.
Tanner staging every 6 months in MAS girls aged 2–7 (peak-risk window for GnRH-independent precocious puberty).
